1.synthesis: In Caesarea lives Cornelius, a centurion of the Italian Cohort—devout, God-fearing, generous to the Jewish poor, and constant in prayer (Acts 10:1–2). An angel appears to him in a vision: 'Your prayers and your alms have ascended as a memorial before God' (Acts 10:4). He is to send to Joppa for Simon called Peter (Acts 10:5–6). Cornelius sends two servants and a devout soldier to Joppa (Acts 10:7–8).
1.historical_context: Caesarea Maritima was the Roman administrative capital of Judea, housing the governor's residence and a significant military garrison. An 'Italian Cohort' (Cohors Italica) stationed in Syria-Palestine is attested in inscriptions from this period. A centurion commanded approximately eighty soldiers. Cornelius is a God-fearer (phobomenos ton theon, v.2)—a Gentile who worshiped the God of Israel, observed Jewish ethical practices, and was welcomed in synagogue life but had not undergone full proselyte conversion (circumcision). His prayers and alms 'ascending as a memorial before God' (v.4) uses the language of the temple incense sacrifice—his worship, though Gentile, has reached the divine presence.
1.literary_structure: The Cornelius episode is the longest conversion narrative in Acts (chapters 10–11) and the theological pivot of the entire book—the moment when the Gentile mission is authorized by divine vision rather than merely human initiative. Luke narrates the event three times (the vision itself, Peter's account to the Jerusalem church in 11:1–18, and Paul's implicit reference in 15:7–11) to emphasize its foundational importance. Cornelius's vision initiates the sequence; Peter's vision responds; their meeting fulfills what both visions prepared. The pattern—divine double-vision leading to human encounter—establishes that the Gentile mission is God's initiative from the beginning.
1.theological_application: Cornelius models the devout pre-Christian seeker who has responded to the light available to them and whose prayers are heard by God. The angel's word—'your prayers and alms have ascended as a memorial before God'—affirms that genuine responsiveness to God within non-Christian frameworks is recognized by the divine, while at the same time sending him for the fuller revelation Peter will bring. This neither collapses the categories (Cornelius's faith is real but incomplete) nor dismisses them (God has been attending to him). The church's mission proceeds as completion and fulfillment of genuine seeking, not displacement of the irrelevant.
1.christology: The angel's instruction to send for Peter introduces a christological necessity: Cornelius's genuine piety needs the proclamation of Jesus as the one through whom God's saving purposes are now accomplished. The memorial of Cornelius's prayers before God prepares him to receive the word about the one whose death and resurrection is the ultimate memorial before God—the sacrifice that supersedes and fulfills all sacrificial memory. The mission to the Gentiles is initiated by the risen Christ who sends his angel to a praying centurion, beginning the extension of salvation to all flesh that the prophets anticipated (Isaiah 49:6).

9.synthesis: The next day, while Cornelius's messengers approach, Peter goes to the roof to pray at noon. He falls into a trance and sees heaven opened, something like a large sheet descending, filled with all kinds of animals, reptiles, and birds (Acts 10:9–12). A voice: 'Rise, Peter; kill and eat.' Peter objects: 'By no means, Lord; for I have never eaten anything that is common or unclean.' The voice: 'What God has made clean, do not call common.' This happens three times; the sheet is taken back to heaven (Acts 10:13–16).
9.historical_context: Peter's vision directly addresses the Jewish purity system that separated kosher from non-kosher food and, by extension, separated Jews from Gentiles in social and table contexts. The sheet filled with every kind of animal represents the full range of Levitically impure creatures. Peter's objection—'I have never eaten anything that is common or unclean'—reflects his genuine, lifelong Torah observance. The threefold repetition of the vision mirrors the threefold denial and threefold restoration; Luke uses the number three as the signal of decisive divine action. Jesus had spoken of food not defiling in Mark 7:19 ('he declared all foods clean'); here the risen Lord applies that teaching to its social and missional implications.
9.literary_structure: The vision's meaning is deliberately withheld while it is given (v.17, 'Peter was inwardly perplexed as to what the vision might mean'), then revealed progressively through the encounter with Cornelius's messengers and finally through the Spirit's explicit instruction (v.19–20). Luke uses delayed interpretation to draw the reader into Peter's own process of understanding. The vision's application (vv.28, 34–35) is social and theological: 'God has shown me that I should not call any person common or unclean' (v.28). Food is the symbol; people are the content.
9.theological_application: The sheet vision is the moment when the entire Levitical purity system is reconfigured in light of the Christ event: the boundary between clean and unclean that organized Jewish social and religious life has been abolished not by human decision but by divine declaration. The church's ongoing engagement with questions of inclusion and exclusion must face the same reconfiguration: what has been called 'unclean' by religious tradition may have been made clean by what God has done in Christ. Peter's initial resistance ('by no means, Lord') and subsequent obedience model the process by which the church receives and applies divine reorientation—resisting the new, then yielding to the persistent divine word.
9.christology: The vision is the risen Christ's application of his own teaching on purity (Mark 7:14–23) to its ecclesiological consequences. By declaring foods clean, Jesus had already in principle declared peoples clean—the Gentile mission is the social outworking of his teaching on the heart as the source of defilement rather than external categories. 'What God has made clean, do not call common' is a divine statement grounded in the work of Christ: through his cross, the partition wall between Jew and Gentile has been abolished (Ephesians 2:14), and the vision rehearses the theological basis for what the mission will enact.

17.synthesis: While Peter ponders the vision, Cornelius's messengers arrive and the Spirit tells him to go with them without hesitation (Acts 10:17–20). Peter invites them in and travels with them to Caesarea, taking some Jewish believers from Joppa (Acts 10:23). Cornelius meets him, falls at his feet; Peter raises him up: 'Stand up; I too am a man' (Acts 10:25–26). Peter explains his arrival: God has shown him not to call any person common or unclean (Acts 10:28). Cornelius explains his vision. Peter opens his mouth: 'Truly I understand that God shows no partiality, but in every nation anyone who fears him and does what is right is acceptable to him' (Acts 10:34–35).
17.historical_context: For a Jewish man to enter the home of a Gentile was ritually transgressive—it would incur contamination requiring purification before temple participation. Peter's explicit acknowledgment (v.28) that this is 'unlawful' (athemiton) for a Jew shows his awareness of what he is doing and that he is doing it under divine authorization. Cornelius's prostration before Peter (v.25) is a Gentile gesture of honor before a divine messenger; Peter's refusal ('I too am a man') mirrors the apostolic pattern in Acts of deflecting divine honor (cf. Acts 14:15). The gathering of Cornelius's relatives and friends (v.24) transforms the encounter into a community event—the household conversion pattern that will characterize Paul's mission.
17.literary_structure: The travel-and-arrival sequence (vv.17–33) prepares for Peter's sermon (vv.34–43) by establishing the divine initiative behind each step: the Spirit commands (v.19–20), the witnesses from Joppa establish community accountability, Cornelius's account confirms the double-vision structure, and Peter's summary statement (vv.34–35) bridges from the vision's content to the proclamation's content. The witnesses from Joppa (v.23) are crucial: they become the evidence Peter will cite to the Jerusalem church in chapter 11.
17.theological_application: 'God shows no partiality' (v.34) is the theological conclusion of the entire double-vision sequence. This is not a new principle—Deuteronomy 10:17 says God shows no partiality—but a new application: impartiality now encompasses Gentiles who fear God and do what is right in every nation. The church's catholicity is grounded in the character of God: the God who does not show favoritism between people groups cannot legitimately be proclaimed only to one people group. The mission to all nations is not an expansion of the church's ambition but a requirement of its theology.
17.christology: Peter's arrival at Cornelius's house is the risen Christ's mission reaching its first Gentile household—not by Paul (who will specialize in Gentile mission) but by Peter (the apostle to the circumcision, Galatians 2:8), so that the Gentile inclusion cannot be attributed to an aberrant strand of early Christianity but is endorsed by the chief apostle. The Christ who directed Cornelius through an angel and directed Peter through a vision is the Lord whose resurrection makes possible this new community across all ethnic and ritual boundaries.

34.synthesis: Peter proclaims the word God sent to Israel, preaching peace through Jesus Christ—who is Lord of all (Acts 10:36). He rehearses the gospel: John's baptism, Jesus anointed with the Spirit and power, going about doing good and healing all oppressed by the devil, crucified and raised on the third day, shown to witnesses who ate and drank with him after the resurrection (Acts 10:37–41). He commanded them to preach and testify that he is the one appointed by God to be judge of the living and the dead. All the prophets testify that everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness of sins through his name (Acts 10:42–43).
34.historical_context: This is the only sermon in Acts addressed to a Gentile audience before Pentecost-like events occur—and it is the most Gospel-shaped of Peter's sermons, following the narrative arc of the Gospel of Mark (Galilee through Judea, baptism, ministry, death, resurrection). The reference to 'Jesus of Nazareth' (v.38) grounds the proclamation in historical particularity. 'Lord of all' (v.36) is the universal scope of the resurrection claim: the one God raised is not merely Israel's Messiah but Lord of all peoples. 'Everyone who believes' (pas ho pisteuōn, v.43) is the first universalist statement in a Petrine sermon, anticipating Paul's formulation in Romans 1:16.
34.literary_structure: The sermon is the briefest of Peter's kerygmatic speeches but the most comprehensive in scope. It has the standard Acts sermon structure: announcement (v.36), historical summary (vv.37–42), and call to response (v.43). The historical summary tracks the Gospel of Luke's narrative: from John through Galilee, to Judea, to death and resurrection. The final verse—'everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness through his name'—is the universal offer that completes the sermon and prompts the Spirit's arrival before Peter can call for baptism.
34.theological_application: Peter's Caesarean sermon is the model of contextualized proclamation to Gentiles: he begins with what Cornelius already knows ('you yourselves know the thing that happened,' v.37) and moves from the known to the unknown, from the historical facts to the theological conclusions. The gospel is both particular (this man, Jesus of Nazareth, in this history) and universal ('Lord of all,' 'everyone who believes'). The proclamation that Jesus is judge of the living and the dead carries urgent ethical weight: the one who forgives is also the one before whom all will stand. The church's proclamation of the gospel must hold together grace (forgiveness) and accountability (judgment).
34.christology: 'Jesus Christ—he is Lord of all' (Acts 10:36) is the sermon's christological summit: the resurrection vindicates not merely Messiahship but universal lordship. The title 'Lord of all' (pantōn kyrios) is applied to God in Romans 10:12—Peter applies it to Jesus, in continuity with the Pentecost sermon's conclusion ('God has made him both Lord and Christ,' Acts 2:36). The one anointed with the Spirit (Acts 10:38) is the same one who appointed as judge of the living and the dead—the complete divine authority of the risen Christ frames every aspect of the mission.

44.pericope_label: The Gentiles Receive the Holy Spirit
44.synthesis: While Peter is still speaking, the Holy Spirit falls on all who hear the word (Acts 10:44). The Jewish believers who came with Peter are astonished—the Spirit has been poured out even on the Gentiles, for they hear them speaking in tongues and praising God (Acts 10:45–46). Peter: 'Can anyone withhold water for baptizing these people, who have received the Holy Spirit just as we have?' He commands them to be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ (Acts 10:47–48).
44.historical_context: The Gentile Pentecost ('as we have,' v.47, echoes the vocabulary of Acts 2) is the crucial theological event that authorizes the Gentile mission—not human decision but divine act. Tongues as the sign of Spirit-reception connects this event to Acts 2 and confirms the Gentile believers' equal standing with the Jerusalem community. Peter's rhetorical question ('can anyone withhold water?') puts the burden of proof on those who would exclude: God has already included them; human institutions must follow. This sequence—Spirit then baptism—reverses the expected order (typically: repentance → baptism → Spirit), making clear that the Spirit's coming is entirely sovereign.
44.literary_structure: The Spirit's falling 'while Peter was still speaking' (v.44) is Luke's signal of divine initiative—the sermon is interrupted by the event it announces. The astonishment of the Jewish witnesses (v.45) is Luke's rhetorical device: their surprise becomes the evidence Peter will cite in Jerusalem ('the Holy Spirit fell on them just as on us at the beginning,' Acts 11:15). The episode closes with commanded baptism—not invitation but command—and an extended stay, suggesting the beginning of community formation in Cornelius's household.
44.theological_application: The Gentile Pentecost resolves the question that the entire Cornelius episode has been building toward: will God receive Gentiles into full membership in the community of the new covenant? The answer comes not through theological debate but through divine action—the Spirit falls before there is opportunity to deliberate. The church's inclusion of previously excluded communities must follow this precedent: when God acts to include, the church's role is to recognize and confirm what God has done, not to grant permission for what God has already authorized. Baptism 'in the name of Jesus Christ' is the community's public seal on the Spirit's private act.
44.christology: The Spirit 'just as on us at the beginning' (Acts 11:15) refers to Pentecost—the gift of the risen and exalted Christ poured out on his community. That the same Pentecostal gift falls on Gentile believers before circumcision or full Torah observance demonstrates that the risen Christ's gift is not mediated through Jewish ethnic identity but given directly to all who receive the proclaimed word. The name of Jesus Christ in which they are baptized (v.48) is the name that is Lord of all—the baptism enacts the lordship claim Peter proclaimed and the Spirit confirmed.
